title: Base word + prefix
teaching_objective: Child spells words made of a prefix in front of an unchanged base word.
child_friendly_explanation: A prefix is a part that goes in FRONT of a base word. It changes the meaning, but it never changes the base word's spelling. Prefix + base — two chunks, snap them together.
rule_explanation: Prefix spelling is fixed and base spelling is preserved: re + play = replay, un + tie = untie. Spell each chunk, then join them with no letters lost.
misconception: Child blends prefix and base by sound and loses letters at the join (unatural for unnatural), or misspells the prefix itself.
watch_for_cue: Two chunks: spell the prefix, spell the base, push them together. Nothing falls off.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Base word + suffix
teaching_objective: Child spells words made of a base word plus a suffix ending.
child_friendly_explanation: A suffix is a part that goes on the END of a base word. It changes the word's job (slow → slowly) but the base usually keeps its spelling. Find the base, spell it, add the ending.
rule_explanation: Suffix spelling is fixed and, by default, base spelling is preserved: slow + ly = slowly, kind + ness = kindness. Spell base then suffix as two chunks.
misconception: Child spells the ending by ear (slowlee, kindniss) or corrupts the base while thinking about the ending.
watch_for_cue: Say the word in two chunks — base, then ending — and spell each chunk.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Find the base word
teaching_objective: Child can find the base word hiding inside a longer word before spelling it.
child_friendly_explanation: Long words are built from a smaller word plus extra parts. Find the small word first — it's the Keeper of the meaning. If you can spell the base, most of the long word is already done.
rule_explanation: A base word is a complete word that can stand alone (help in helpful, play in replaying). Spot the base first, spell it, then add the extra parts around it.
misconception: Child sounds out the whole long word letter by letter and never notices the small word inside, so the base gets mangled (helful, replaing).
watch_for_cue: Before you spell a long word, ask: what small word is hiding inside?
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Keep the base word's spelling
teaching_objective: Child keeps the base word's spelling unchanged when parts are added.
child_friendly_explanation: When you add a part to a word, the base word usually keeps every one of its letters. The Keeper doesn't drop letters just because a new part arrives.
rule_explanation: Base spelling is usually preserved when affixes are added: hope + less = hopeless, joy + ful = joyful. (Some suffix rules change the ending — that's a different skill — but the default is: keep the base whole.)
misconception: Child drops or changes letters at the join (hopless, joyfull), especially where two of the same letter meet (unnecessary, really).
watch_for_cue: Spell the base first, then bolt the part on — don't lose any letters at the join, even if it makes a double letter.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Closed compound words
teaching_objective: Child can spell compounds written as one word, preserving both bases.
child_friendly_explanation: Some words are two whole words glued into one: foot + ball = football, rain + bow = rainbow. Both little words keep ALL their letters — two Keepers, one word.
rule_explanation: Closed compounds join two complete base words with no letters lost, even where letters double up at the join: book + keeper = bookkeeper.
misconception: Child drops letters at the join (foot+ball → 'footbal') or spells the whole thing by sound instead of finding the two words.
watch_for_cue: Find BOTH little words first. Spell each one whole, then push them together — nothing falls off at the join.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Open and hyphenated compounds
teaching_objective: Child can spell compounds written as separate words or with a hyphen, keeping parts and spacing correct.
child_friendly_explanation: Some two-word teams stay apart (ice cream), and some hold hands with a hyphen (well-known). The words themselves stay perfectly spelled — the skill is knowing whether they're glued, hyphenated, or separate. Hyphenated compound: Join the words with a hyphen when they work together as one describing idea. Open compound: Leave a space when the two words belong together but are usually written separately.
rule_explanation: Open compounds keep a space (ice cream, living room); hyphenated compounds join with - (twenty-one, part-time). Each word part keeps its full spelling.
misconception: Child glues open compounds into one word (icecream), drops the hyphen, or hyphenates randomly.
watch_for_cue: Spell each word perfectly first; then ask — glued, hyphen, or space? Learn each team's style like a name.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved with amended child-friendly explanation for open and hyphenated compounds. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The prefixes dis- and mis-
teaching_objective: Child can spell words with dis- (not/opposite) and mis- (wrongly) and keep the base intact.
child_friendly_explanation: dis- means NOT or the OPPOSITE (disagree = not agree). mis- means WRONGLY or BADLY (misspell = spell wrongly). Both keep their own letters AND the base keeps its letters.
rule_explanation: dis- and mis- are fixed spellings before an unchanged base: dis + appear = disappear (one s from dis, keep the base), mis + spell = misspell (s from mis + s from spell = ss).
misconception: Child writes one s where two belong (mispell) or two where one belongs (dissapear), because they can't hear the join.
watch_for_cue: You can't HEAR the join — you have to BUILD it: mis + spell = misspell (ss); dis + appear = disappear (one s, two p's from the base).
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The changing prefix in-, im-, il-, ir-
teaching_objective: Child can choose the right form of the negative prefix in-/im-/il-/ir- by looking at the base word's first letter.
child_friendly_explanation: This 'not' prefix is a shape-shifter: it copies the start of the base so it's easier to say. Before most letters it's in- (invisible). Before m or p it's im- (impossible). Before l it's il- (illegal). Before r it's ir- (irregular).
rule_explanation: The negative prefix assimilates to the base's first letter: im- before m/p, il- before l, ir- before r, in- elsewhere. This often creates a double letter: il + legal = illegal, ir + regular = irregular.
misconception: Child uses in- everywhere (inpossible, inlegal), or writes a single letter where the assimilated double belongs (ilegal, iregular).
watch_for_cue: Look at the FIRST letter of the base — the prefix copies it: l → il (illegal, ll), r → ir (irregular, rr), m/p → im.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The prefixes re- and pre-
teaching_objective: Child can spell words with re- (again) and pre- (before) and tell them apart by meaning.
child_friendly_explanation: re- means AGAIN (replay = play again). pre- means BEFORE (preheat = heat before). Both are two-letter parts that snap onto the front of a base word without changing it.
rule_explanation: re- and pre- are fixed spellings attached before an unchanged base: re + build = rebuild, pre + view = preview. Choose the prefix by meaning: again → re-, before → pre-.
misconception: Child confuses which prefix carries which meaning, or spells them by ear (ri-, per-, pri-).
watch_for_cue: Ask the meaning first: is it 'again' (re-) or 'before' (pre-)? Then spell the base unchanged.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The prefixes sub-, inter-, super-
teaching_objective: Child can spell longer Latin prefixes sub- (under), inter- (between) and super- (above/beyond) as stable chunks.
child_friendly_explanation: These are bigger front-parts from Latin, each with a picture-meaning: sub- = UNDER (submarine goes under the sea), inter- = BETWEEN (international = between nations), super- = ABOVE or MORE THAN (superhero).
rule_explanation: sub-, inter- and super- are fixed multi-letter chunks before an unchanged base: sub + way, inter + net, super + market. Spell the whole prefix as one known chunk.
misconception: Child spells the prefix by ear (supper-, intre-, sup-) or breaks it into sounds and loses letters.
watch_for_cue: Learn each prefix as ONE picture-chunk: under = sub-, between = inter-, above = super-. Then add the base whole.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The prefix un-
teaching_objective: Child can spell words with the prefix un- and knows it means 'not' or 'opposite of'.
child_friendly_explanation: un- is a tiny part that means NOT or the OPPOSITE. Stick it on the front and the word flips: kind → unkind, lock → unlock. The base word keeps every letter.
rule_explanation: un- is always spelled u-n, attached before a base word whose spelling stays the same: un + happy = unhappy, un + necessary = unnecessary (double n stays).
misconception: Child drops a letter at the join, especially double letters (unecessary, unatural), or spells the prefix by ear (on-, an-).
watch_for_cue: un- is always just u-n, and the base keeps all its letters — even if that makes nn.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Common Greek roots
teaching_objective: Child can recognise and spell common Greek roots, including their special letter patterns (ph, ch, y).
child_friendly_explanation: The ancient Greeks gave us word-treasures too: tele = far (telephone, telescope), phon = sound, graph = write/draw, photo = light. Greek roots carry secret signs — ph says /f/, ch says /k/ — that mark a word as Greek.
rule_explanation: Greek roots keep stable spelling and distinctive graphemes: phon (sound), graph (write), photo (light), tele (far), scope (look), micro (small). ph=/f/ and ch=/k/ signal Greek origin.
misconception: Child spells Greek graphemes by ear (telefone, fotograf, kemist) missing the ph/ch signature.
watch_for_cue: An /f/ in a science-y word is often the Greek sign ph — telephone, graph, photo. Spot the Greek treasure marks.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Common Latin roots
teaching_objective: Child can recognise and spell common Latin roots as stable chunks inside longer words.
child_friendly_explanation: Two thousand years ago the Romans spoke Latin — and their word-bricks are still inside our words! port = carry (transport, export, import). struct = build (structure, construct). Spot the Roman brick and half the word is spelled.
rule_explanation: Latin roots keep stable spelling across word families: port (carry), struct (build), rupt (break), tract (pull), dict (say), scrib/script (write). Spell root as a fixed chunk, then add affixes.
misconception: Child sounds out through the root and corrupts it (transpot, constuct), missing the stable chunk.
watch_for_cue: Find the Roman brick first — port, struct, rupt — spell it as one chunk, then add the front and end parts.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Root families keep their spelling
teaching_objective: Child can hold a root's spelling steady across its whole word family, even as endings and sounds change.
child_friendly_explanation: A root is the family surname — it's spelled the same on every family member. act: act, actor, action, active, react. The endings change, the sounds shift, but a-c-t never breaks.
rule_explanation: Root spelling is invariant across derivatives: act/action/actor, medic/medical/medicine, sign/signal/signature. Anchor the root, then vary the affixes.
misconception: Child re-spells the root differently in each family member (acshun, medisin) instead of anchoring it once.
watch_for_cue: Learn the surname once — act, medic, sign — and trust it in every relative, whatever the ending sounds like.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Science and maths roots
teaching_objective: Child can use Greek and Latin roots to spell subject words in science and maths.
child_friendly_explanation: School subject words look scary until you see the bricks: thermometer = therm (heat) + meter (measure). Triangle = tri (three) + angle. Once you know ten bricks, you can spell (and understand!) a hundred subject words.
rule_explanation: Subject vocabulary is built from productive roots: meter (measure), therm (heat), bio (life), geo (earth), tri (three), cent (hundred), poly (many), hydro (water). Spell root-by-root.
misconception: Child treats each long subject word as a brand-new random string and sounds through it (thurmomiter, trianggle).
watch_for_cue: Break the scary word into bricks you know: therm + o + meter. Spell each brick — the word spells itself.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The suffixes -able and -ible
teaching_objective: Child can spell -able and -ible (can be done) and use the base-word test to choose between them.
child_friendly_explanation: Both endings mean CAN BE: washable = can be washed, visible = can be seen. The detective test: if you remove the ending and a whole real word is left, it's usually -able (wash → washable). If what's left isn't a word by itself, it's usually -ible (vis → visible).
rule_explanation: -able typically follows complete base words (enjoyable, comfortable); -ible typically follows bound Latin roots (possible, terrible, incredible). The test guides, with exceptions learned as tricky words.
misconception: Child guesses by sound — the two endings sound identical (schwa) — writing washible or visable.
watch_for_cue: You can't hear the difference — run the test: take the ending off. Real word left? -able. Not a word? probably -ible.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The suffix -al
teaching_objective: Child can spell -al and knows it makes describing words meaning 'to do with'.
child_friendly_explanation: -al is an ADJECTIVE-MAKER meaning 'to do with': music → musical, nation → national. Always a-l, even when it sounds like 'ul' or 'le'.
rule_explanation: -al is a fixed ending forming adjectives from nouns, spelled -al (never -le/-el/-ul in these built words): accident + al = accidental.
misconception: Child spells the ending by ear (-le, -el, -ul: musicle, nashunal) because the vowel is a schwa.
watch_for_cue: If you can strip it back to a real base word (music, nation, accident), the ending is -al.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The suffixes -ful and -less
teaching_objective: Child can spell -ful (full of) and -less (without) and knows they are opposites.
child_friendly_explanation: -ful means FULL OF (hopeful = full of hope). -less means WITHOUT (hopeless = without hope). They're opposite twins that snap onto the same bases. Big trap: -ful has ONE l, even though it means 'full'.
rule_explanation: -ful is always spelled with a single l (careful, not carefull); -less always has double s. Base is preserved: care + ful, care + less.
misconception: Child writes -full with two l's because the meaning is 'full', or -les with one s.
watch_for_cue: The suffix -ful lost an l on its journey: full of care = careful, ONE l. And -less always keeps its ss.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The suffix -ity
teaching_objective: Child can spell -ity and knows it turns describing words into naming words.
child_friendly_explanation: -ity is a NOUN-MAKER: active → activity, real → reality. It names the quality. It sounds like 'ittee' but it's always i-t-y.
rule_explanation: -ity is a fixed ending forming abstract nouns from adjectives; spelled -ity, never -aty/-etty. Related words share the spelling: real → reality.
misconception: Child spells the weak vowel by ear (activaty, realety) because the i is unstressed.
watch_for_cue: The middle vowel is a whisper — but its family gives it away: real → reality, both spelled with the base intact, then i-t-y.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The suffix -ment
teaching_objective: Child can spell -ment and knows it turns a doing word into a naming word.
child_friendly_explanation: -ment is a NOUN-MAKER for actions: enjoy → enjoyment (the thing you feel), pay → payment (the thing you pay). It's always m-e-n-t and the base stays whole.
rule_explanation: -ment is a fixed ending added to verbs to form nouns, with the base preserved: treat + ment = treatment, move + ment = movement (the e stays!).
misconception: Child spells it by ear (-mant, -mint) or drops the base's final e (movment, excitment).
watch_for_cue: Base first, whole and complete — movement keeps the e of move — then add m-e-n-t.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The suffix -ness
teaching_objective: Child can spell -ness and knows it turns a describing word into a naming word (a state or quality).
child_friendly_explanation: -ness is a NOUN-MAKER. Stick it on a describing word and you get the name of that feeling or state: kind → kindness, dark → darkness. It's always n-e-s-s.
rule_explanation: -ness is a fixed ending (never -nes or -niss) added to adjectives to form nouns. Base is preserved by default: sad + ness = sadness. (y→i bases like happiness follow the y-rule, taught separately.)
misconception: Child spells the ending by ear (-nes, -niss, -nis) or doubles wrongly at the join.
watch_for_cue: -ness is ALWAYS n-e-s-s — four letters, no exceptions. Spell the base first, then add all four.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: The suffix -ous
teaching_objective: Child can spell -ous and knows it makes describing words meaning 'full of'.
child_friendly_explanation: -ous is an ADJECTIVE-MAKER meaning full of: danger → dangerous (full of danger), fame → famous. The tricky bit: it SOUNDS like 'us' but it's spelled o-u-s.
rule_explanation: -ous is a fixed ending forming adjectives; it is never spelled -us or -is. Common pattern: base + ous with base largely preserved (dangerous, mountainous).
misconception: Child spells the ending as it sounds (-us: dangerus, famus) because the o is a lazy schwa sound.
watch_for_cue: That ending sounds like 'us' but the Keeper knows it's o-u-s — three letters, o first.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Word families with sound shifts
teaching_objective: Child can preserve family spelling even when pronunciation changes across relatives.
child_friendly_explanation: Sometimes relatives SOUND different but LOOK the same: nature/natural, please/pleasant, know/knowledge. The sound shifts — the spelling stays loyal to the family.
rule_explanation: English preserves morphological spelling across pronunciation shifts. Trust the family spelling, not the changed sound: pleasant keeps the ea of please.
misconception: Child re-spells the shifted relative by its new sound (plesant, nachural) instead of keeping the family letters.
watch_for_cue: When a relative sounds different, don't panic-spell by ear — the family letters stay the same.
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.

title: Use a related word to spell
teaching_objective: Child can use a clearly-spelled relative to fix a hidden or unclear letter.
child_friendly_explanation: Words come in families, and a relative can tell you the secret letter. Can't hear the g in sign? Its cousin SIGNAL says it loudly. Not sure about muscle? MUSCULAR shouts the hidden c... the family keeps the spelling.
rule_explanation: Related words share meaning-spelling: sign/signal/signature, know/knowledge, medic/medicine. When a letter is silent or unclear, find a family member that pronounces it.
misconception: Child spells the tricky word by sound alone (sine, mussel) and never thinks to call in a relative.
watch_for_cue: Stuck on a silent or fuzzy letter? Ask the family: which relative says that letter out loud?
reviewer_comment: Human-approved for D4_MOR category-v1 candidate source. Approval recorded 2026-07-11.
